density matrix.content:
﻿Title: Nielsen & Chuang Core Concepts
Source(s): Nielsen, M. A. & Chuang, I. L., "Quantum Computation and Quantum Information" (2010)
Authors: Michael A. Nielsen; Isaac L. Chuang (summarized)
Year: 2010
Domain: Quantum Foundations / Quantum Information
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Qubit: Two-level quantum system represented by state vector $|\psi\rangle=\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle$.
- Density operator: $\rho$ describing mixed states; $\rho=\sum_i p_i|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i|$.
- Unitary evolution: Closed-system dynamics described by $\rho\mapsto U\rho U^{\dagger}$.
- Measurement (projective): Born rule $p_i=\langle\psi|\Pi_i|\psi\rangle$ for projector $\Pi_i$.
- Entanglement: Non-separable joint states with correlations beyond classical mixtures.

Key Concepts
- State space: Hilbert space formalism and tensor-product composition.
- Quantum gates: Reversible operations implemented by unitary matrices.
- Quantum circuits: Composition of gates to implement algorithms.
- Quantum error: Decoherence and noise motivating error correction.

Light equation context
- State normalization: $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle=1$.
- Expectation value: $\langle A\rangle=\mathrm{Tr}(\rho A)$.

Concept explanations
- Superposition: Linear combination of basis states enabling interference.
- Measurement collapse (operational): Probabilistic update of state post-measurement.
- Quantum tomography (summary): Reconstruction of $\rho$ from measurement statistics.

Citations
- Nielsen & Chuang (2010). Core textbook treatment of these foundational topics.

grover algorithm.content:
Title: Quantum Algorithms Overview
Source(s): 
• Shor, P. W. (1994) Algorithms for quantum computation: discrete logarithms and factoring.
• Grover, L. K. (1996) A fast quantum mechanical algorithm for database search.
• Nielsen & Chuang (2010) Quantum Computation and Quantum Information.
• Farhi et al. (2014) A Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (arXiv:1411.4028).
• Peruzzo et al. (2014) A variational eigenvalue solver on a quantum processor.

Authors: Curated summary
Year: 2026 (ingestion)
Domain: Quantum Algorithms
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Quantum algorithm: Algorithm exploiting quantum mechanical phenomena (superposition, interference, entanglement) to solve computational problems.
- Oracle: Abstract subroutine used in many algorithmic formulations (e.g., Grover's search).

Key Points
- Quantum speedups: Proven exponential (Shor) and quadratic (Grover) advantages for specific problems.
- Algorithm classes: Factoring and period-finding, unstructured search, simulation, optimization (VQE, QAOA).
- Resource considerations: Qubit count, gate depth, error rates, and classical-quantum hybrid steps.

Concept explanations
- Shor's algorithm: Period-finding with quantum Fourier transform yields integer factoring speedup (cite Shor 1994).
- Grover's search: Amplitude amplification producing $O(\sqrt{N})$ queries for unstructured search.
- Quantum simulation: Using quantum systems to simulate quantum chemistry and materials; basis for many near-term use cases.
- Variational algorithms: Hybrid quantum-classical optimization using parameterized circuits (VQE, QAOA) for noisy hardware.

Light equation context
- Grover iteration amplitude scaling: After $k$ iterations, success amplitude scales approximately as $\sin((2k+1)\theta)$ where $\sin(\theta)=1/\sqrt{N}$.
- Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT): Linear transform mapping computational basis to frequency domain; implemented with $O(n^2)$ gates in textbook circuits.

Title: Quantum Algorithms Overview
Source(s): 
• Shor, P. W. (1994) Algorithms for quantum computation: discrete logarithms and factoring.
• Grover, L. K. (1996) A fast quantum mechanical algorithm for database search.
• Nielsen & Chuang (2010) Quantum Computation and Quantum Information.
• Farhi et al. (2014) A Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (arXiv:1411.4028).
• Peruzzo et al. (2014) A variational eigenvalue solver on a quantum processor.

Citations
• Shor (1994) – Factoring algorithm.
• Grover (1996) – Quantum search.
• Farhi et al. (2014) – QAOA.
• Peruzzo et al. (2014) – VQE.
• Nielsen & Chuang (2010) – Circuit model and QFT.

readme.content:
# UniGuru Quantum Knowledge Base Expansion

This repository contains documentation-only knowledge base expansion
for the UniGuru system.

## Scope
This work expands the knowledge/quantum with foundational and advanced
quantum computing material using canonical textbooks, peer-reviewed
papers, and university lecture notes.

## Important Constraints
This repository contains:
• Read-only documentation
• Non-executable content
• No runtime or behavioral changes
• No tools or agents

## Domains Covered
• Quantum Computation & Information
• Quantum Algorithms
• Quantum Information Theory
• Quantum Hardware
• Quantum Software Toolchains
• Quantum Mathematics
• Quantum Biology
• Quantum Chemistry

## Sources
Canonical textbooks, peer-reviewed research papers, university
lecture notes, and arXiv publications.

## Purpose
Knowledge Base Deepening Phase for UniGuru.

Definitions
- Quantum biology: Study of quantum phenomena in biological systems and their functional significance.
- Exciton: Bound electron-hole pair important in energy transport in photosynthetic complexes.

Key Points
- Investigated phenomena: Quantum coherence in photosynthetic energy transfer, avian magnetoreception hypotheses, and tunneling in enzymatic reactions.
- Evidence and debate: Experimental signatures of short-lived coherence exist; functional relevance remains an active research area.

Concept explanations
- Photosynthetic complexes: Coherent exciton transport can enhance energy transfer under certain conditions; models use open quantum systems techniques.
- Magnetoreception: Radical-pair mechanism proposes spin-dependent chemical reactions as a compass.

Light equation context
- Open system dynamics: Master equations (Lindblad form) model environment-driven decoherence in biological contexts.

Citations
- Selected peer-reviewed reviews on quantum effects in biology (see journal articles and reviews).

Definitions
- Electronic structure problem: Determining ground and excited states of molecular Hamiltonians.
- Second quantization: Formalism representing fermionic creation/annihilation operators for many-body systems.

Key Points
- Quantum simulation: Mapping molecular Hamiltonians to qubit operators for simulation on quantum hardware.
- Algorithms: VQE and quantum phase estimation (QPE) are central approaches for chemistry problems.
- Resource challenges: Hamiltonian sparsity, fermion-to-qubit encodings (Jordan–Wigner, Bravyi–Kitaev), and circuit depth.

Concept explanations
- VQE: Variational hybrid algorithm optimizing parameterized circuits to approximate ground states with classical optimizers.
- QPE: High-precision eigenvalue estimation requiring deeper circuits and error-corrected hardware.

Light equation context
- Molecular Hamiltonian (second quantized form): $H=\\sum_{pq} h_{pq} a_p^{\\dagger} a_q + \\frac{1}{2} \\sum_{pqrs} h_{pqrs} a_p^{\\dagger} a_q^{\\dagger} a_r a_s$ (summary form).

Citations
- Reviews on quantum algorithms for chemistry and landmark papers on VQE and QPE.

Definitions
- Qubit modality: Physical implementation of a qubit (superconducting, trapped-ion, photonic, spin).
- Coherence time: Timescale over which a qubit preserves quantum phase information (T1, T2).
- Gate fidelity: Accuracy of implemented quantum gates relative to ideal unitaries.

Key Points
- Major platforms: Superconducting circuits, trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and spin defects.
- Performance metrics: Qubit count, coherence times, gate fidelities, connectivity, and readout accuracy.
- Scaling challenges: Crosstalk, control electronics, cryogenics, system integration, and error correction overhead.

Concept explanations
- Superconducting qubits: Fast gates and integration with microwave control; cryogenic operation.
- Trapped ions: Long coherence, high-fidelity gates, slower gate speeds relative to superconducting.
- Photonic approaches: Room-temperature operation for certain tasks; advantages for communication.

Light equation context
- Error rates: Infidelity often expressed as $1-F$ where $F=|\mathrm{Tr}(U^{\dagger}V)|^2/\text{dim}(U)$ for ideal $U$ and implemented $V$.

Citations
- Reviews and hardware-specific papers: platform overviews in peer-reviewed journals.

Title: Quantum Information Core
Source(s): Source(s):

• Preskill Lecture Notes – Quantum Computation (Caltech)

• Shor (1995) Scheme for reducing decoherence in quantum memory.

Authors: Curated summary
Year: 2026 (ingestion)
Domain: Quantum Information Theory
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Von Neumann entropy: $S(\rho)=-\mathrm{Tr}(\rho \log \rho)$ measuring quantum uncertainty.
- Mutual information: $I(A:B)=S(\rho_A)+S(\rho_B)-S(\rho_{AB})$.
- Holevo bound: Limits accessible classical information from quantum states.
- No-cloning theorem: Unknown quantum states cannot be copied exactly.

Key Points
- Entropy and information measures generalize classical definitions to quantum ensembles.
- Quantum channels: Completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) maps model noise and communication.
- Entanglement measures: Concurrence, entanglement entropy, and operational tasks (teleportation, dense coding).

Concept explanations
- Quantum communication: Uses entanglement and quantum channels for tasks like teleportation and QKD.
- Error correction: Quantum error-correcting codes protect logical qubits via redundancy and syndrome measurement.

Light equation context
- Channel action: $\rho\mapsto\sum_k E_k\rho E_k^{\dagger}$ with $\sum_k E_k^{\dagger}E_k=I$ for CPTP maps.
Quantum Teleportation
Quantum teleportation enables transfer of an unknown quantum state using entanglement and classical communication.
Protocol components:
• Shared Bell pair
• Bell-basis measurement
• Classical communication of 2 bits
• Conditional Pauli corrections

Quantum Error Correction
Quantum error correction protects logical qubits from decoherence.
Core principles:
• Redundancy via encoding
• Syndrome measurement without collapsing logical state
• Fault-tolerant operations

Examples:
• Shor code
• Steane code
• Surface code


Citations
- Nielsen & Chuang (2010); Preskill lecture notes (quantum information sections); peer-reviewed sources on specific topics.

Title: Quantum Mathematics Foundations
Source(s): Source(s):

Definitions
- Hilbert space: Complete inner-product space over complex numbers used for quantum states.
- Linear operator: Maps between vectors in Hilbert space; observables are Hermitian operators.
- Tensor product: Formalism for composite systems $\mathcal{H}_{AB}=\mathcal{H}_A\otimes\mathcal{H}_B$.

Key Points
- Linear algebra is central: eigenvalue problems, diagonalization, singular-value decomposition.
- Operator algebra: Commutators $[A,B]=AB-BA$ and their role in uncertainty relations.
- Spectral theorem: Self-adjoint operators have real eigenvalues and an orthonormal eigenbasis.

Concept explanations
- Basis and representation: Choice of basis affects matrix representation but not physical predictions.
- Partial trace: Operation to obtain reduced states: $\rho_A=\mathrm{Tr}_B(\rho_{AB})$.

Light equation context
- Uncertainty (Robertson): $\Delta A\,\Delta B\ge\tfrac{1}{2}|\langle[A,B]\rangle|$.

Citations
- Standard linear algebra and functional analysis references; Nielsen & Chuang for applied context.

Definitions
- Quantum SDK: Software development kit providing abstractions to build, transpile, and run quantum circuits.
- Compiler/transpiler: Transforms logical circuits into hardware-native instructions respecting connectivity and gate sets.
- Simulator: Classical emulation of quantum circuits for testing and benchmarking.

Key Points
- Stack layers: Algorithms → Circuit design → Compilation/transpilation → Pulse/control → Hardware.
- Tooling considerations: Noise-aware compilation, error mitigation, profiling, and benchmarking.
- Hybrid workflows: Classical optimizers interacting with parameterized quantum circuits.

Concept explanations
- Transpilation: Mapping logical gates to native gate set and qubit topology with resource-aware optimizations.
- Error mitigation: Techniques (zero-noise extrapolation, probabilistic error cancellation) to improve results on noisy hardware.

Light equation context
- Circuit fidelity estimation: Model combining gate error rates and decoherence to predict output quality.

Citations
- SDK and compiler literature; peer-reviewed papers on compilation and error mitigation.

Definitions
- Electronic structure problem: Determining ground and excited states of molecular Hamiltonians.
- Second quantization: Formalism representing fermionic creation/annihilation operators for many-body systems.

Key Points
- Quantum simulation: Mapping molecular Hamiltonians to qubit operators for simulation on quantum hardware.
- Algorithms: VQE and quantum phase estimation (QPE) are central approaches for chemistry problems.
- Resource challenges: Hamiltonian sparsity, fermion-to-qubit encodings (Jordan–Wigner, Bravyi–Kitaev), and circuit depth.

Concept explanations
- VQE: Variational hybrid algorithm optimizing parameterized circuits to approximate ground states with classical optimizers.
- QPE: High-precision eigenvalue estimation requiring deeper circuits and error-corrected hardware.

Light equation context
- Molecular Hamiltonian (second quantized form): $H=\\sum_{pq} h_{pq} a_p^{\\dagger} a_q + \\frac{1}{2} \\sum_{pqrs} h_{pqrs} a_p^{\\dagger} a_q^{\\dagger} a_r a_s$ (summary form).

Citations
- Reviews on quantum algorithms for chemistry and landmark papers on VQE and QPE.

nielsen chuang core.content:
﻿Title: Nielsen & Chuang Core Concepts
Source(s): Nielsen, M. A. & Chuang, I. L., "Quantum Computation and Quantum Information" (2010)
Authors: Michael A. Nielsen; Isaac L. Chuang (summarized)
Year: 2010
Domain: Quantum Foundations / Quantum Information
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Qubit: Two-level quantum system represented by state vector $|\psi\rangle=\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle$.
- Density operator: $\rho$ describing mixed states; $\rho=\sum_i p_i|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i|$.
- Unitary evolution: Closed-system dynamics described by $\rho\mapsto U\rho U^{\dagger}$.
- Measurement (projective): Born rule $p_i=\langle\psi|\Pi_i|\psi\rangle$ for projector $\Pi_i$.
- Entanglement: Non-separable joint states with correlations beyond classical mixtures.

Key Concepts
- State space: Hilbert space formalism and tensor-product composition.
- Quantum gates: Reversible operations implemented by unitary matrices.
- Quantum circuits: Composition of gates to implement algorithms.
- Quantum error: Decoherence and noise motivating error correction.

Light equation context
- State normalization: $\langle\psi|\psi\rangle=1$.
- Expectation value: $\langle A\rangle=\mathrm{Tr}(\rho A)$.

Concept explanations
- Superposition: Linear combination of basis states enabling interference.
- Measurement collapse (operational): Probabilistic update of state post-measurement.
- Quantum tomography (summary): Reconstruction of $\rho$ from measurement statistics.

Citations
- Nielsen & Chuang (2010). Core textbook treatment of these foundational topics.

- Von Neumann entropy: $S(\rho)=-\mathrm{Tr}(\rho \log \rho)$ measuring quantum uncertainty.

- Holevo bound: Limits accessible classical information from quantum states.

Key Points
- Entropy and information measures generalize classical definitions to quantum ensembles.

- Quantum communication: Uses entanglement and quantum channels for tasks like teleportation and QKD.
- Error correction: Quantum error-correcting codes protect logical qubits via redundancy and syndrome measurement.

- Channel action: $\rho\mapsto\sum_k E_k\rho E_k^{\dagger}$ with $\sum_k E_k^{\dagger}E_k=I$ for CPTP maps.
Quantum Teleportation
Quantum teleportation enables transfer of an unknown quantum state using entanglement and classical communication.
Protocol components:
• Shared Bell pair
• Bell-basis measurement
• Classical communication of 2 bits
• Conditional Pauli corrections

Quantum Error Correction
Quantum error correction protects logical qubits from decoherence.
Core principles:
• Redundancy via encoding
• Syndrome measurement without collapsing logical state
• Fault-tolerant operations

Examples:
• Shor code
• Steane code
• Surface code


Citations
- Nielsen & Chuang (2010); Preskill lecture notes (quantum information sections); peer-reviewed sources on specific topics.

# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Vachanamrut (Complete Core)

---
title: Vachanamrut — The Principal Philosophical Text of Swaminarayan Sampradaya
source: Vachanamrut, Bhagwan Swaminarayan (compiled by Muktanand Swami et al.)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/vachanamrut
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Vachanamrut (Vachana = Word, Amrut = Nectar) is the principal scripture of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya. It is a compilation of 273 discourses delivered by Bhagwan Swaminarayan between 1819–1829 CE, transcribed by his disciples and compiled into a canonical text.

**Structure:**
The 273 discourses are organized by location:
- Gadhada (I, II, III) — 76 + 67 + 39 discourses
- Sarangpur — 18 discourses
- Kariyani — 12 discourses
- Loya — 18 discourses
- Panchala — 7 discourses
- Vartal — 20 discourses
- Amdavad — 3 discourses
- Jetalpur — 4 discourses
- Bhugol-Khagol (extra additions)

**Key Philosophical Doctrines:**
1. **Panch Vartman** — Five moral vows: abstinence from meat, intoxicants, theft, adultery, and impurity
2. **Upasana** — The understanding that Swaminarayan is Purushottam (Supreme Being), par-kar (possessing divine form), sarvopari (supreme above all devis/devatas), and pragat (ever-present)
3. **The Four Entities** — Para-brahma (Supreme God), Akshara-brahma (eternal abode), maya (illusion), and jivas (individual souls)

**Gadhada I-1 (The First Discourse):**
Swaminarayan asks: "What is the characteristic of an 'ekantik-bhakta'?"
Answer: One who possesses the four pillars of dharma, jnan, vairagya, and bhakti is an ekantik-bhakta (ultimate devotee).

UniGuru Citation: Vachanamrut, Gadhada I-1; BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha, Vachanamrut Study Edition

Title: Quantum Hardware Overview
Source(s):
• Preskill, J. (2018) Quantum Computing in the NISQ era and beyond.
• Krantz et al. (2019) A quantum engineer’s guide to superconducting qubits.
• Blatt & Wineland (2008) Entangled states of trapped ions.
• O’Brien et al. (2009) Photonic quantum technologies.
ted 
Authors: Curated summary
Year: 2026 (ingestion)
Domain: Quantum Hardware
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Qubit modality: Physical implementation of a qubit (superconducting, trapped-ion, photonic, spin).
- Coherence time: Timescale over which a qubit preserves quantum phase information (T1, T2).
- Gate fidelity: Accuracy of implemented quantum gates relative to ideal unitaries.

Key Points
- Major platforms: Superconducting circuits, trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and spin defects.
- Performance metrics: Qubit count, coherence times, gate fidelities, connectivity, and readout accuracy.
- Scaling challenges: Crosstalk, control electronics, cryogenics, system integration, and error correction overhead.

Concept explanations
- Superconducting qubits: Fast gates and integration with microwave control; cryogenic operation.
- Trapped ions: Long coherence, high-fidelity gates, slower gate speeds relative to superconducting.
- Photonic approaches: Room-temperature operation for certain tasks; advantages for communication.

Light equation context
- Error rates: Infidelity often expressed as $1-F$ where $F=|\mathrm{Tr}(U^{\dagger}V)|^2/\text{dim}(U)$ for ideal $U$ and implemented $V$.

Citations
- Reviews and hardware-specific papers: platform overviews in peer-reviewed journals.

quantum math foundations.content:
Title: Quantum Mathematics Foundations
Source(s): Source(s):
• Nielsen & Chuang (2010)
• Axler – Linear Algebra Done Right
• Hall – Quantum Theory for Mathematicians

Authors: Curated summary
Year: 2026 (ingestion)
Domain: Mathematical foundations for quantum theory
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Hilbert space: Complete inner-product space over complex numbers used for quantum states.
- Linear operator: Maps between vectors in Hilbert space; observables are Hermitian operators.
- Tensor product: Formalism for composite systems $\mathcal{H}_{AB}=\mathcal{H}_A\otimes\mathcal{H}_B$.

Key Points
- Linear algebra is central: eigenvalue problems, diagonalization, singular-value decomposition.
- Operator algebra: Commutators $[A,B]=AB-BA$ and their role in uncertainty relations.
- Spectral theorem: Self-adjoint operators have real eigenvalues and an orthonormal eigenbasis.

Concept explanations
- Basis and representation: Choice of basis affects matrix representation but not physical predictions.
- Partial trace: Operation to obtain reduced states: $\rho_A=\mathrm{Tr}_B(\rho_{AB})$.

Light equation context
- Uncertainty (Robertson): $\Delta A\,\Delta B\ge\tfrac{1}{2}|\langle[A,B]\rangle|$.

Citations
- Standard linear algebra and functional analysis references; Nielsen & Chuang for applied context.

Title: Quantum Software Stack
Source(s): 
• Qiskit documentation (IBM Quantum)
• Cirq documentation (Google Quantum AI)
• PennyLane documentation (Xanadu)
• Häner et al. (2018) Software methodology for compiling quantum programs.

Authors: Curated summary
Year: 2026 (ingestion)
Domain: Quantum Software / Tooling
Ingestion date: 2026-02-06

Definitions
- Quantum SDK: Software development kit providing abstractions to build, transpile, and run quantum circuits.
- Compiler/transpiler: Transforms logical circuits into hardware-native instructions respecting connectivity and gate sets.
- Simulator: Classical emulation of quantum circuits for testing and benchmarking.

Key Points
- Stack layers: Algorithms → Circuit design → Compilation/transpilation → Pulse/control → Hardware.
- Tooling considerations: Noise-aware compilation, error mitigation, profiling, and benchmarking.
- Hybrid workflows: Classical optimizers interacting with parameterized quantum circuits.

Concept explanations
- Transpilation: Mapping logical gates to native gate set and qubit topology with resource-aware optimizations.
- Error mitigation: Techniques (zero-noise extrapolation, probabilistic error cancellation) to improve results on noisy hardware.

Light equation context
- Circuit fidelity estimation: Model combining gate error rates and decoherence to predict output quality.

Citations
- SDK and compiler literature; peer-reviewed papers on compilation and error mitigation.

nyaya logic.content:
Nyaya is the school of logic and epistemology in Indian philosophy. 
It defines four valid sources of knowledge (Pramanas):
1. Pratyaksha (Perception)
2. Anumana (Inference)
3. Upamana (Comparison)
4. Shabda (Word/Testimony)

Logical reasoning involves a five-membered syllogism (Avayava):
- Pratijna (Proposition)
- Hetu (Reason)
- Udaharana (Example)
- Upanaya (Application)
- Nigamana (Conclusion)

nyaya.content:
Nyaya is the school of logic and epistemology in Indian philosophy. 
It defines four valid sources of knowledge (Pramanas):
1. Pratyaksha (Perception)
2. Anumana (Inference)
3. Upamana (Comparison)
4. Shabda (Word/Testimony)

Logical reasoning involves a five-membered syllogism (Avayava):
- Pratijna (Proposition)
- Hetu (Reason)
- Udaharana (Example)
- Upanaya (Application)
- Nigamana (Conclusion)

panchavayava argument.content:
The classical Nyaya presentation of inference is often described through five parts:
1. Pratijna: the thesis or proposition.
2. Hetu: the reason offered in support of the thesis.
3. Udaharana: the example that demonstrates the rule.
4. Upanaya: the application of that rule to the current case.
5. Nigamana: the conclusion drawn from the reasoning.

This structure helps students distinguish a supported inference from an unsupported claim.

acharanga sutra.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Acharanga Sutra

---
title: Acharanga Sutra — The First Agama of Jainism
source: Acharanga Sutra (Āyāraṃga Sutta), Jain Agama Texts
url: https://www.sacred-texts.com/jai/sbe22/index.htm
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Āyāraṃga Sutta (Acharanga Sutra) is the first and oldest of the Jain Agamas, the canonical texts of Shvetambara Jainism. It prescribes the conduct of a Jain ascetic and is a primary source for understanding Mahavira's teachings.

**Core Teaching — Non-Violence (Ahimsa):**
"All beings desire to live. None wishes to die. Therefore, a wise person does not kill, nor causes others to kill, nor consents to killing."

**Five Great Vows (Mahavrata) for Jain Monks:**
1. **Ahimsa** — Non-violence toward all living beings
2. **Satya** — Absolute truthfulness
3. **Asteya** — Non-stealing
4. **Brahmacharya** — Celibacy
5. **Aparigraha** — Complete non-possessiveness

**Mahavira's Enlightenment:**
Mahavira practiced severe austerities for 12 years and 6 months. He attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) under a Sal tree by the bank of the Rijupalika River.

**Social Equality:**
The Acharanga Sutra records that Mahavira rejected caste distinctions, teaching that all souls are equal regardless of birth.

UniGuru Citation: Acharanga Sutra, Book 1, Lecture 7; Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22, Jacobi translation

# Jain Knowledge Base — Tattvartha Sutra (Expanded)

---
title: Tattvartha Sutra — The Complete Path to Liberation
source: Tattvartha Sutra by Acharya Umaswati (Umāsvāti)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/tattvartha-sutra
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Tattvartha Sutra (also spelled Tattvārthasūtra) is the foundational philosophical text of Jainism, composed by Acharya Umaswati around the 2nd–5th century CE. It is unique in being accepted by all three main Jain sects.

**Sutra 1.1:** "Samyag-darshana-jnana-charitrani mokshamargah"
Translation: Right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct together constitute the path to liberation (Moksha).

**The Seven Tattvas (Cosmic Realities):**
1. **Jiva** — Soul (sentient being with consciousness)
2. **Ajiva** — Non-soul (matter, time, space, dharma, adharma)
3. **Asrava** — Inflow of karmic matter into the soul
4. **Bandha** — Bondage — karma binding to the soul
5. **Samvara** — Stoppage of karma inflow
6. **Nirjara** — Shedding of karma (through austerity)
7. **Moksha** — Final liberation; the soul's complete freedom

These seven principles form the complete metaphysical framework of Jain philosophy.

UniGuru Citation: Acharya Umaswati, Tattvartha Sutra, Sutra 1.1, Chapter 1

# Jain Knowledge Base — Uttaradhyayana Sutra

---
title: Uttaradhyayana Sutra — Mahavira's Final Teachings
source: Uttarādhyayana Sūtra, Jain Agama — Shvetambara Canon
url: https://www.sacred-texts.com/jai/sbe45/index.htm
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Uttaradhyayana Sutra (Uttarādhyayana Sūtra) contains 36 chapters of Mahavira's last discourses delivered to his disciples just before his nirvana (liberation). It is among the most revered of the Jain scriptural texts.

**The Four Gatis (States of Existence):**
1. **Naraka** — Hell realms
2. **Tiryanca** — Plants and animals
3. **Manushya** — Human beings
4. **Deva** — Heavenly beings

**Key Teaching on Karma:**
"The soul becomes what it does. Actions of violence, deceit, and possessiveness bind the soul to the cycle of death and rebirth (samsara). The path out is right conduct."

**On the Supremacy of Right Conduct (Chapter 1):**
Mahavira teaches that even a king who neglects virtue and dharma is inferior to a pauper who lives righteously.

**Lesha Dhyana (Meditation Classifications):**
The text describes four types of meditation:
- Arta-dhyana (sorrowful meditation) — leads to lower births
- Raudra-dhyana (cruel meditation) — leads to hells
- Dharma-dhyana (righteous meditation) — leads to liberation path
- Shukla-dhyana (pure meditation) — the meditation of liberated souls

UniGuru Citation: Uttaradhyayana Sutra, Chapter 1, Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 45, Jacobi translation

anekantavada syadvada.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Anekantavada and Syadvada

---
title: Anekantavada and Syadvada — Jain Doctrine of Relative Truth
source: Tattvarthasutra; Pramana-nayatattvalokalankara (Akalanka)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/anekantavada
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Anekantavada (Non-one-sidedness) is one of the most important philosophical contributions of Jainism to Indian thought. It holds that ultimate reality is complex and cannot be described from a single viewpoint.

**Core Principle:**
Reality has infinite aspects. Any statement we make captures only one aspect (naya). Claiming absolute truth from one perspective alone is called Ekanta (one-sidedness) and is considered an error.

**Syadvada (Conditional Predication — the "Maybe" Doctrine):**
Every statement about reality must be qualified with "syat" (in some sense / from some perspective). The seven forms:
1. Syāt asti — "In some sense, it is"
2. Syāt nāsti — "In some sense, it is not"
3. Syāt asti-nāsti — "In some sense, it both is and is not"
4. Syāt avaktavya — "In some sense, it is indescribable"
5. Syāt asti-avaktavya — "In some sense, it is and is indescribable"
6. Syāt nāsti-avaktavya — "In some sense, it is not and is indescribable"
7. Syāt asti-nāsti-avaktavya — "In some sense, it is, is not, and is indescribable"

**Nayavada (Doctrine of Standpoints):**
Every claim is valid from a particular standpoint (naya). Jain logic distinguishes:
- Dravyarthika naya (substance standpoint)
- Paryayarthika naya (modal/particular standpoint)

**Significance:**
Anekantavada provides a framework for intellectual tolerance and is seen as the philosophical underpinning of Ahimsa — non-violence in thought as well as action.

UniGuru Citation: Tattvarthasutra; Akalanka's Laghiyastraya; Paul Dundas, "The Jains" (2002)

jain cosmology.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Jain Cosmology

---
title: Jain Cosmology — Loka, Time Cycles, and the Universe
source: Jain Agamas; Tiloyapannatti; Tattvarthasutra Chapter 3-4
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/cosmology
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Jain cosmology presents a detailed, non-theistic description of the universe (Loka) as eternal, uncreated, and self-sustaining.

**The Loka (Universe):**
The Jain universe is shaped like a cosmic human figure. It is divided into:
- **Adho Loka** (Lower World) — Seven levels of hell (naraka)
- **Madhya Loka** (Middle World) — Where humans, animals, and plants exist
- **Urdhva Loka** (Upper World) — 16 heavens and Siddhashila at the apex

**Siddhashila:**
The Siddhashila is the topmost point of the universe — a crescent-shaped region where all liberated souls (Siddhas) reside in perfect, eternal bliss and omniscience. No karma can reach them.

**Cyclic Time (Kala Chakra):**
Time moves in a wheel with ascending (Utsarpini) and descending (Avasarpini) half-cycles. We are currently in the 5th spoke (Ara) of the descending half:
- Truth gradually diminishes
- Human lifespan and virtue decline
- The next Tirthankara will appear in the next Utsarpini cycle

**Jain Tirthankaras:**
There are 24 Tirthankaras per half-cycle. The first is Rishabhadeva (Adinatha) and the last in our cycle is Mahavira. All other deities, including Indra, are below the Tirthankaras in spiritual status.

**Atoms and Matter:**
Jain atomism (paramanu) holds the universe is composed of ultimate indivisible particles which combine to form all material objects including karma.

UniGuru Citation: Tattvarthasutra Chapter 3 (Cosmology), Tiloyapannatti (Yativrsabha)

# Jain Knowledge Base — Karma Doctrine

---
title: Jain Karma Theory — The Eight Types of Karma
source: Tattvarthasutra Chapters 6-8; Karmagranthas (Devendrasuri)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/karma-doctrine
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Jain karma theory is one of the most elaborate and systematic karma philosophies in Indian religion. Unlike Hindu Vedantic karma, Jain karma is treated as a physical substance — fine particles of matter that bind to the soul.

**Karma as Matter:**
Karma (karmana) consists of infinitely fine material particles that flow into the soul through mental, verbal, and physical activities (yoga) combined with passions (kashaya).

**Eight Types of Karma (Ashta Karma):**
1. **Jnanavaraniya** — Knowledge-obscuring karma (blocks omniscience)
2. **Darshanavaraniya** — Perception-obscuring karma (blocks pure perception)
3. **Vedaniya** — Feeling-producing karma (produces pleasure and pain)
4. **Mohaniya** — Deluding karma (the most dangerous — causes wrong belief and passions)
5. **Ayu** — Lifespan-determining karma (determines the duration of a life)
6. **Nama** — Body-determining karma (determines physical form, species, and attributes)
7. **Gotra** — Status-determining karma (determines family/social standing)
8. **Antaraya** — Obstructing karma (blocks charity, gain, enjoyment, and spiritual power)

**Asrava (Karma Inflow Causes):**
- Wrong belief (mithyatva)
- Non-restraint (avirati)
- Negligence (pramada)
- Passions (kashaya): anger, pride, deceit, greed
- Activities of mind, body, speech (yoga)

**Nirjara (Karma Shedding):**
Karma falls away naturally over time (savipaka nirjara) or can be forcibly shed through deliberate austerities (avipaka nirjara/tapas).

UniGuru Citation: Tattvarthasutra Chapters 6, 7, 8; Karmagranthas, Devendrasuri

# Jain Knowledge Base — Jain Ethics and Lay Conduct

---
title: Jain Ethics — Twelve Vratas for Lay Practitioners (Shravaka Dharma)
source: Tattvartha Sutra Chapter 7; Ratnakaranda Shravakachara (Samantabhadra)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/shravaka-dharma
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Jain ethics prescribes a graduated path of conduct for both ascetic monks and lay practitioners (shravaka/shravika). For laypeople, twelve minor vows (vratas) guide ethical life.

**Five Anuvratas (Minor Vows):**
1. Ahimsa Anuvrata — Avoid intentional killing of all mobile beings
2. Satya Anuvrata — Avoid deliberate lying
3. Asteya Anuvrata — Avoid theft and accepting what is not given
4. Brahmacharya Anuvrata — Fidelity to one's spouse; abstain from unchaste conduct
5. Aparigraha Anuvrata — Limit possessions to necessity

**Three Guna Vratas (Supportive Vows):**
6. Digvrata — Limit the geographical area of one's activities (reduce harm by scope)
7. Bhogopabhoga Parimana — Limit daily consumption of consumable and non-consumable items
8. Anartha Danda Viramana — Avoid pointless harmful activities (gambling, hunting, debauchery)

**Four Shiksha Vratas (Disciplinary Vows):**
9. Samayika — Practice equanimity meditation daily
10. Desavagasika — Further restrict movement for specific periods
11. Poshadha — Fast on festival days and observe monk-like conduct
12. Atithi Samvibhaga — Share food, medicines, and knowledge with worthy ascetics

**The Goal:**
Following these vows gradually purifies the soul, reduces karma inflow, and places the layperson on the path toward eventual renunciation and liberation.

UniGuru Citation: Tattvartha Sutra Chapter 7; Ratnakaranda Shravakachara, Samantabhadra

8. **Antaraya** — Obstructing karma (blocks charity, gain, enjoyment, and spiritual power)

**Asrava (Karma Inflow Causes):**
- Wrong belief (mithyatva)
- Non-restraint (avirati)
- Negligence (pramada)
- Passions (kashaya): anger, pride, deceit, greed
- Activities of mind, body, speech (yoga)

**Nirjara (Karma Shedding):**
Karma falls away naturally over time (savipaka nirjara) or can be forcibly shed through deliberate austerities (avipaka nirjara/tapas).

UniGuru Citation: Tattvarthasutra Chapters 6, 7, 8; Karmagranthas, Devendrasuri

doctrine.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Karma Doctrine

---
title: Jain Karma Theory — The Eight Types of Karma
source: Tattvarthasutra Chapters 6-8; Karmagranthas (Devendrasuri)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/karma-doctrine
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Jain karma theory is one of the most elaborate and systematic karma philosophies in Indian religion. Unlike Hindu Vedantic karma, Jain karma is treated as a physical substance — fine particles of matter that bind to the soul.

**Karma as Matter:**
Karma (karmana) consists of infinitely fine material particles that flow into the soul through mental, verbal, and physical activities (yoga) combined with passions (kashaya).

**Eight Types of Karma (Ashta Karma):**
1. **Jnanavaraniya** — Knowledge-obscuring karma (blocks omniscience)
2. **Darshanavaraniya** — Perception-obscuring karma (blocks pure perception)
3. **Vedaniya** — Feeling-producing karma (produces pleasure and pain)
4. **Mohaniya** — Deluding karma (the most dangerous — causes wrong belief and passions)
5. **Ayu** — Lifespan-determining karma (determines the duration of a life)
6. **Nama** — Body-determining karma (determines physical form, species, and attributes)
7. **Gotra** — Status-determining karma (determines family/social standing)
8. **Antaraya** — Obstructing karma (blocks charity, gain, enjoyment, and spiritual power)

**Asrava (Karma Inflow Causes):**
- Wrong belief (mithyatva)
- Non-restraint (avirati)
- Negligence (pramada)
- Passions (kashaya): anger, pride, deceit, greed
- Activities of mind, body, speech (yoga)

**Nirjara (Karma Shedding):**
Karma falls away naturally over time (savipaka nirjara) or can be forcibly shed through deliberate austerities (avipaka nirjara/tapas).

UniGuru Citation: Tattvarthasutra Chapters 6, 7, 8; Karmagranthas, Devendrasuri

# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Satpurush: The Living Saint Doctrine

---
title: Satpurush — The Doctrine of the Living Saint in Swaminarayan Philosophy
source: Vachanamrut; Swamini Vato; BAPS Akshar-Purushottam Darshan
url: https://www.baps.org/satpurush-doctrine
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The doctrine of the Satpurush (Satpurusha = True/Perfect Person/Saint) is central to the spiritual path in the Swaminarayan Sampradaya, particularly within BAPS.

**Definition:**
A Satpurush is not merely a spiritual teacher (guru) but the living embodiment of Akshar-Brahman on Earth — a soul who has completely transcended maya (illusion) and whose consciousness is merged with Purushottam's divine abode.

**Why the Living Satpurush is Essential:**
According to the Vachanamrut (Gadhada III-10), Bhagwan Swaminarayan explained:
"Only through the living Satpurush can a jiva become brahmarup and thereby gain the ability to truly worship me. Without this, all worship remains within the realm of maya."

**Characteristics of the Satpurush:**
1. Complete freedom from all material attachment (lobh, krodh, kama, mana, matsarya)
2. 24-hour divine consciousness — never forgets God even in sleep
3. Absolute identification with Akshar and service to Purushottam
4. Natural compassion and the ability to spiritually uplift others
5. No ego — recognizes himself as a servant, not a master

**The Guru-Disciple Relationship (Upasana):**
A disciple's relationship with the Satpurush must involve:
- Physical service (tana seva)
- Financial service (dhana seva)  
- Mental devotion (mana seva)
- When all three are surrendered completely, the disciple attains brahmarup

**Progressive Succession:**
BAPS holds that the Akshar-Brahman successively inhabits each of its Brahmaswarup successors, ensuring that the living gateway to Purushottam is never absent from the Earth as long as the Sampradaya continues.

UniGuru Citation: Vachanamrut Gadhada III-10, Gadhada I-71; Swamini Vato 6.40; BAPS Akshar-Purushottam Darshan

jain shravaka ethics.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Jain Ethics and Lay Conduct

---
title: Jain Ethics — Twelve Vratas for Lay Practitioners (Shravaka Dharma)
source: Tattvartha Sutra Chapter 7; Ratnakaranda Shravakachara (Samantabhadra)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/shravaka-dharma
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Jain ethics prescribes a graduated path of conduct for both ascetic monks and lay practitioners (shravaka/shravika). For laypeople, twelve minor vows (vratas) guide ethical life.

**Five Anuvratas (Minor Vows):**
1. Ahimsa Anuvrata — Avoid intentional killing of all mobile beings
2. Satya Anuvrata — Avoid deliberate lying
3. Asteya Anuvrata — Avoid theft and accepting what is not given
4. Brahmacharya Anuvrata — Fidelity to one's spouse; abstain from unchaste conduct
5. Aparigraha Anuvrata — Limit possessions to necessity

**Three Guna Vratas (Supportive Vows):**
6. Digvrata — Limit the geographical area of one's activities (reduce harm by scope)
7. Bhogopabhoga Parimana — Limit daily consumption of consumable and non-consumable items
8. Anartha Danda Viramana — Avoid pointless harmful activities (gambling, hunting, debauchery)

**Four Shiksha Vratas (Disciplinary Vows):**
9. Samayika — Practice equanimity meditation daily
10. Desavagasika — Further restrict movement for specific periods
11. Poshadha — Fast on festival days and observe monk-like conduct
12. Atithi Samvibhaga — Share food, medicines, and knowledge with worthy ascetics

**The Goal:**
Following these vows gradually purifies the soul, reduces karma inflow, and places the layperson on the path toward eventual renunciation and liberation.

UniGuru Citation: Tattvartha Sutra Chapter 7; Ratnakaranda Shravakachara, Samantabhadra

mahavira life.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Mahavira: Life and Teachings

---
title: Mahavira — Life, Renunciation, and Enlightenment
source: Jain Agamas (Acharanga Sutra, Kalpa Sutra); Encyclopedia of Jainism
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/mahavira-biography
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Vardhamana Mahavira (599–527 BCE) is the 24th and last Tirthankara (ford-maker/enlightened teacher) of the current cosmic cycle in Jain cosmology.

**Birth and Lineage:**
Born in Vaishali (present-day Bihar, India) to King Siddhartha and Queen Trishala of the Licchavi clan. He is also known as Jina ("the conqueror") — conqueror of inner enemies: anger, pride, deceit, and greed.

**Renunciation:**
At age 30, Mahavira renounced his royal life, worldly possessions, and family, becoming a wandering ascetic monk. He tore out his hair in five handfuls (kesha-loch) rather than shaving with a razor — the traditional Jain initiation.

**Twelve Years of Austerity:**
For 12 years and 6 months, Mahavira observed extreme austerities. He did not harm even insects while walking. He fasted frequently and endured harsh conditions.

**Enlightenment (Kevala Jnana):**
At age 42, under a Sal tree in Jrimbhikagrama, Mahavira attained Kevala Jnana — omniscience, the highest form of knowledge in Jainism — which perceives all time, all space, all beings simultaneously.

**Nirvana:**
Mahavira attained Moksha (liberation) at Pavapuri, Bihar, at age 72. The night of his Nirvana is celebrated as Diwali by Jains.

**Core Message:**
"Parasparopagraho jivanam" — All life is mutually supportive. Live and let live.

UniGuru Citation: Kalpa Sutra (Bhadrabahu), Life of Mahavira sections; Acharanga Sutra Book 1-2

rishabhadeva adinatha.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Rishabhadeva (Adinatha)

---
title: Rishabhadeva — The First Tirthankara
source: Ādi Purāṇa (Jinasena); Kalpa Sutra; Rishabhdev Foundation
url: https://www.jainworld.com/rishabhadeva
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

Rishabhadeva (also called Adinatha — "First Lord") is the first of the 24 Tirthankaras of the current cosmic time cycle according to Jain cosmology.

**Mythological Status:**
He is credited as the founder of human civilization:
- Taught humans to cook and eat food (previously humans ate only tubers and fruit)
- Introduced agriculture, crafts, and governance
- Established the first kingdom and social order

**Renunciation and Tapas:**
After ruling for countless years, Rishabhadeva renounced his kingdom and became a naked ascetic (digambara). He meditated without eating for one year and eventually attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience).

**Mention in Other Traditions:**
The Bhagavata Purana (a Hindu text) describes Rishabha as an avatara of Vishnu who taught yoga and renunciation — demonstrating Jain influence on broader Indian religious tradition.

**Symbol:**
Rishabhadeva's symbol is the Bull (Vrishabha/nandi). His yaksha (attendant deity) is Gomukha and his yakshi is Chakreshvari.

**Ashta Pratiharya (Eight Auspicious Events at Enlightenment):**
When a Tirthankara attains Kevala Jnana, eight supernatural manifestations appear including celestial music, fragrant rain, and divine light.

**Legacy:**
Rishabhadeva's teachings were preserved through his 84 disciples (ganadharas) of whom Pundarika was the chief.

UniGuru Citation: Adi Purana (Jinasena); Kalpa Sutra of Bhadrabahu, Life of Rishabhadeva section

sutrakritanga.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Sutrakritanga

---
title: Sutrakritanga — Critique of Other Philosophies and Jain Ethics
source: Sūtrakṛtāṅga, Second Jain Agama, Shvetambara Canon
url: https://www.sacred-texts.com/jai/sbe45/index.htm
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Sūtrakṛtāṅga is the second of the 12 Angas (main canonical texts) of Jainism. It contrasts Jain philosophy with other contemporary philosophical schools and provides guidance on ethical conduct for monks.

**Critique of Materialism (Lokayata):**
The text refutes the Cārvāka (materialist) school which held that only the material world exists and that the soul does not survive death. Jainism asserts the soul (jiva) is eternal and not reducible to matter.

**Critique of Fatalism (Ajivika):**
The Ajivikas taught that all events are pre-determined. The Sutrakritanga argues that human effort (purushakara) is essential and that karma can be modified by right action.

**The Doctrine of Anekantavada (Many-Sidedness of Truth):**
Jain epistemology holds that truth is multi-faceted. No single perspective can capture the complete reality of any entity. This is expressed through the Syādvāda (conditional predication):
- "In some sense, a thing is." (Syāt asti)
- "In some sense, a thing is not." (Syāt nāsti)
- "In some sense, a thing both is and is not." (Syāt asti-nāsti)

**Protection of All Lifeforms:**
The text classifies living beings by their number of senses (1 to 5) and teaches that even one-sensed beings (plants, microorganisms) have a right to life.

UniGuru Citation: Sutrakritanga, Book 1, Lecture 1; Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 45

tattvartha sutra.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Tattvartha Sutra (Expanded)

---
title: Tattvartha Sutra — The Complete Path to Liberation
source: Tattvartha Sutra by Acharya Umaswati (Umāsvāti)
url: https://www.jainfoundation.in/tattvartha-sutra
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Tattvartha Sutra (also spelled Tattvārthasūtra) is the foundational philosophical text of Jainism, composed by Acharya Umaswati around the 2nd–5th century CE. It is unique in being accepted by all three main Jain sects.

**Sutra 1.1:** "Samyag-darshana-jnana-charitrani mokshamargah"
Translation: Right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct together constitute the path to liberation (Moksha).

**The Seven Tattvas (Cosmic Realities):**
1. **Jiva** — Soul (sentient being with consciousness)
2. **Ajiva** — Non-soul (matter, time, space, dharma, adharma)
3. **Asrava** — Inflow of karmic matter into the soul
4. **Bandha** — Bondage — karma binding to the soul
5. **Samvara** — Stoppage of karma inflow
6. **Nirjara** — Shedding of karma (through austerity)
7. **Moksha** — Final liberation; the soul's complete freedom

These seven principles form the complete metaphysical framework of Jain philosophy.

UniGuru Citation: Acharya Umaswati, Tattvartha Sutra, Sutra 1.1, Chapter 1

7. **Moksha** — Final liberation; the soul's complete freedom

These seven principles form the complete metaphysical framework of Jain philosophy.

UniGuru Citation: Acharya Umaswati, Tattvartha Sutra, Sutra 1.1, Chapter 1

uttaradhyayana sutra.content:
# Jain Knowledge Base — Uttaradhyayana Sutra

---
title: Uttaradhyayana Sutra — Mahavira's Final Teachings
source: Uttarādhyayana Sūtra, Jain Agama — Shvetambara Canon
url: https://www.sacred-texts.com/jai/sbe45/index.htm
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: jain
---

## Content

The Uttaradhyayana Sutra (Uttarādhyayana Sūtra) contains 36 chapters of Mahavira's last discourses delivered to his disciples just before his nirvana (liberation). It is among the most revered of the Jain scriptural texts.

**The Four Gatis (States of Existence):**
1. **Naraka** — Hell realms
2. **Tiryanca** — Plants and animals
3. **Manushya** — Human beings
4. **Deva** — Heavenly beings

**Key Teaching on Karma:**
"The soul becomes what it does. Actions of violence, deceit, and possessiveness bind the soul to the cycle of death and rebirth (samsara). The path out is right conduct."

**On the Supremacy of Right Conduct (Chapter 1):**
Mahavira teaches that even a king who neglects virtue and dharma is inferior to a pauper who lives righteously.

**Lesha Dhyana (Meditation Classifications):**
The text describes four types of meditation:
- Arta-dhyana (sorrowful meditation) — leads to lower births
- Raudra-dhyana (cruel meditation) — leads to hells
- Dharma-dhyana (righteous meditation) — leads to liberation path
- Shukla-dhyana (pure meditation) — the meditation of liberated souls

UniGuru Citation: Uttaradhyayana Sutra, Chapter 1, Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 45, Jacobi translation

akshar purushottam darshan.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Akshar-Purushottam Darshan (BAPS)

---
title: Akshar-Purushottam Darshan — Theological Doctrine of BAPS
source: Vachanamrut; Swamini Vato (Gunatitanand Swami); BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha
url: https://www.baps.org/akshar-purushottam-darshan
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

Akshar-Purushottam Darshan is the central theological philosophy of the BAPS (Bochasanwasi Akshar Purushottam Sanstha) school within the Swaminarayan Sampradaya.

**The Two Supreme Entities:**
1. **Purushottam** — Supreme God (Bhagwan Swaminarayan), the ultimate reality, highest divinity, controller of all
2. **Akshar (Aksharbrahman)** — The eternal, divine abode of Purushottam AND the ever-present perfect devotee who guides others to liberation

**The Akshar-Brahman on Earth:**
A central BAPS doctrine holds that Akshar is not only the divine abode but also manifests on Earth in human form as the living Brahmaswarup saint who acts as the gateway to Purushottam. In BAPS succession:
- Gunatitanand Swami (d. 1867)
- Bhagatji Maharaj (d. 1897)
- Shastriji Maharaj (d. 1951)
- Yogiji Maharaj (d. 1971)
- Pramukh Swami Maharaj (d. 2016)
- Mahant Swami Maharaj (current)

**The Philosophical Framework:**
The universe contains five eternal entities:
1. Jiva (individual souls)
2. Ishvara (divine controllers of creation)
3. Maya (primordial nature/illusion)
4. Akshar (divine abode/gateway)
5. Purushottam (Supreme God — Swaminarayan)

**Path to Liberation (Moksha):**
One attains moksha only by becoming Brahmarup (divine in consciousness) through association with and devotion to the living Akshar-Brahman. Simply following rituals or other paths is considered the "indirect path."

UniGuru Citation: Vachanamrut Gadhada III-38; Swamini Vato; BAPS Akshar-Purushottam Darshan publication

mandirs akshardham.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Swaminarayan Temples and Akshardham

---
title: Swaminarayan Mandirs — Architecture, Worship, and Akshardham
source: BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha; Swaminarayan Akshardham official documentation
url: https://www.baps.org/mandirs
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Swaminarayan Sampradaya places great importance on the construction of mandirs (temples) as sacred spaces where Bhagwan Swaminarayan is believed to be physically present.

**Original Six Mandirs:**
Bhagwan Swaminarayan himself consecrated six mandirs during his lifetime:
1. Ahmedabad (1822)
2. Bhuj (1822)
3. Vadtal (1824)
4. Dholera (1826)
5. Junagadh (1828)
6. Gadhada (1828)

These are still considered the most sacred mandirs and remain under the two dioceses (Ahmedabad and Vadtal) established by Swaminarayan himself.

**Swaminarayan Akshardham, Delhi:**
Dedicated in 2005, the Akshardham monument in New Delhi is recognized by the Guinness World Records as the world's largest comprehensive Hindu temple complex.
Key features:
- Main monument: 141 ft high, 316 ft wide, 356 ft long
- Constructed in pink sandstone and marble with no steel reinforcement
- 20,000 carved figures, 234 ornate pillars
- Built by 11,000 artisans and 3,000 BAPS volunteers over 5 years

**Swaminarayan Akshardham, Gandhinagar, Gujarat:**
The original Akshardham, built by Pramukh Swami Maharaj in 1992.

**Akshar-dholi:**
Sadhus (BAPS monks) do not receive salaries and live entirely according to Ekantik Dharma — absolute celibacy, non-violence, non-possessiveness, and complete devotion to Purushottam.

**Global Presence:**
BAPS maintains over 1,100 mandirs worldwide across 55 countries (as of 2024).

UniGuru Citation: BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha official documentation; Guinness World Records (2007 entry)

nishkulanand swami.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Nishkulanand Swami and Devotional Poetry

---
title: Nishkulanand Swami — Devotional Poet and Theologian
source: Bhaktachintamani, Purushottam Prakash, Chosath Pad (Nishkulanand Swami)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/nishkulanand-swami
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

Sadguru Nishkulanand Swami (1766–1848 CE) is one of the most revered saint-poets of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya. He is celebrated for documenting the life and miracles of Bhagwan Swaminarayan through devotional poetry and prose.

**Major Works:**
1. **Bhaktachintamani** — The most complete hagiography of Bhagwan Swaminarayan, compiled in verse form
2. **Purushottam Prakash** — Philosophical poem on the divine nature of Purushottam
3. **Chosath Pad** — 64 devotional songs on vairagya (renunciation)
4. **Yama Danda** — Poetic description of consequences of sinful actions

**Philosophy of Vairagya (Renunciation):**
Nishkulanand Swami's writings emphasize that true renunciation is internal — detachment from the ego and from the desire for worldly pleasures, reputation, and relationships. 

*Famous Opening of Chosath Pad:*
"Vairagya tenu kahu vadhu, je dekhade nij dosh" 
("That is true renunciation which reveals one's own faults and shortcomings.")

**Importance of Satsang:**
He emphasizes the company of true saints (satsang) as the most important method of spiritual advancement. Even one moment of satsang with the Brahmaswarup saint can transform the soul.

**Historical Significance:**
His Bhaktachintamani is considered the equivalent of Valmiki's Ramayana for the Swaminarayan Sampradaya — the primary literary source for Swaminarayan's biography and divine activities.

UniGuru Citation: Nishkulanand Swami, Bhaktachintamani (complete edition); Chosath Pad, Verse 1

panch vartman.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Panch Vartman (Five Moral Vows)

---
title: Panch Vartman — The Five Fundamental Vows of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya
source: Vachanamrut; Shikshapatri (Bhagwan Swaminarayan, 1826)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/panch-vartman
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Panch Vartman (Five Vows / Five Codes of Conduct) are the foundational ethical commitments required of all initiated members of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya.

**The Five Vows:**

1. **Nishpapa (Ahimsa / Non-harm)**
   Abstain from violence. Do not harm any living being unnecessarily. This prohibits meat eating for all initiates.

2. **Nirmatsari (Non-intoxication)**
   Complete abstinence from alcohol, drugs, tobacco, and intoxicating substances of all kinds.

3. **Nischaurya (Non-theft)**
   Do not steal or take anything that has not been given with consent. Act with complete honesty in all dealings.

4. **Nishkam (Celibacy in mind and action)**
   Maintaining purity of thought, word, and deed. For householders this means fidelity; for sadhus it means complete celibacy.

5. **Nirlobha (Non-possessiveness / Non-greed)**
   Detachment from material possessions, wealth, and status. Seeking only what is necessary and dedicating all resources to God's service.

**Purpose:**
These five vows purify the individual's character and remove the obstacles to developing genuine devotion (bhakti) to Purushottam. They are prerequisites for deeper spiritual progress.

**Initiation (Diksha):**
When a devotee formally commits to the Panch Vartman at initiation, they receive kanthi (a tulsi wood beaded necklace) as a mark of initiation.

**Universal Application:**
Unlike caste-based Hindu ethical codes, the Panch Vartman applies equally to all members regardless of gender, caste, or social status — a socially revolutionary position for 19th century India.

UniGuru Citation: Shikshapatri Verses 9-16; Vachanamrut Gadhada I-18; BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha ethical framework

# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Panch Vartman (Five Moral Vows)

---
title: Panch Vartman — The Five Fundamental Vows of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya
source: Vachanamrut; Shikshapatri (Bhagwan Swaminarayan, 1826)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/panch-vartman
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Panch Vartman (Five Vows / Five Codes of Conduct) are the foundational ethical commitments required of all initiated members of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya.

**The Five Vows:**

1. **Nishpapa (Ahimsa / Non-harm)**
   Abstain from violence. Do not harm any living being unnecessarily. This prohibits meat eating for all initiates.

2. **Nirmatsari (Non-intoxication)**
   Complete abstinence from alcohol, drugs, tobacco, and intoxicating substances of all kinds.

3. **Nischaurya (Non-theft)**
   Do not steal or take anything that has not been given with consent. Act with complete honesty in all dealings.

4. **Nishkam (Celibacy in mind and action)**
   Maintaining purity of thought, word, and deed. For householders this means fidelity; for sadhus it means complete celibacy.

5. **Nirlobha (Non-possessiveness / Non-greed)**
   Detachment from material possessions, wealth, and status. Seeking only what is necessary and dedicating all resources to God's service.

**Purpose:**
These five vows purify the individual's character and remove the obstacles to developing genuine devotion (bhakti) to Purushottam. They are prerequisites for deeper spiritual progress.

**Initiation (Diksha):**
When a devotee formally commits to the Panch Vartman at initiation, they receive kanthi (a tulsi wood beaded necklace) as a mark of initiation.

**Universal Application:**
Unlike caste-based Hindu ethical codes, the Panch Vartman applies equally to all members regardless of gender, caste, or social status — a socially revolutionary position for 19th century India.

UniGuru Citation: Shikshapatri Verses 9-16; Vachanamrut Gadhada I-18; BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha ethical framework

satpurush doctrine.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Satpurush: The Living Saint Doctrine

---
title: Satpurush — The Doctrine of the Living Saint in Swaminarayan Philosophy
source: Vachanamrut; Swamini Vato; BAPS Akshar-Purushottam Darshan
url: https://www.baps.org/satpurush-doctrine
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The doctrine of the Satpurush (Satpurusha = True/Perfect Person/Saint) is central to the spiritual path in the Swaminarayan Sampradaya, particularly within BAPS.

**Definition:**
A Satpurush is not merely a spiritual teacher (guru) but the living embodiment of Akshar-Brahman on Earth — a soul who has completely transcended maya (illusion) and whose consciousness is merged with Purushottam's divine abode.

**Why the Living Satpurush is Essential:**
According to the Vachanamrut (Gadhada III-10), Bhagwan Swaminarayan explained:
"Only through the living Satpurush can a jiva become brahmarup and thereby gain the ability to truly worship me. Without this, all worship remains within the realm of maya."

**Characteristics of the Satpurush:**
1. Complete freedom from all material attachment (lobh, krodh, kama, mana, matsarya)
2. 24-hour divine consciousness — never forgets God even in sleep
3. Absolute identification with Akshar and service to Purushottam
4. Natural compassion and the ability to spiritually uplift others
5. No ego — recognizes himself as a servant, not a master

**The Guru-Disciple Relationship (Upasana):**
A disciple's relationship with the Satpurush must involve:
- Physical service (tana seva)
- Financial service (dhana seva)  
- Mental devotion (mana seva)
- When all three are surrendered completely, the disciple attains brahmarup

**Progressive Succession:**
BAPS holds that the Akshar-Brahman successively inhabits each of its Brahmaswarup successors, ensuring that the living gateway to Purushottam is never absent from the Earth as long as the Sampradaya continues.

UniGuru Citation: Vachanamrut Gadhada III-10, Gadhada I-71; Swamini Vato 6.40; BAPS Akshar-Purushottam Darshan

shikshapatri.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Shikshapatri

---
title: Shikshapatri — Code of Conduct Written by Bhagwan Swaminarayan
source: Shikshapatri, composed by Bhagwan Swaminarayan, 1826 CE
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/shikshapatri
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Shikshapatri (Shiksha = Teaching, Patri = Letter) is a code of conduct composed by Bhagwan Swaminarayan in Sanskrit on Maha Sud 5, 1882 VS (February 11, 1826 CE). It contains 212 Sanskrit shlokas (verses) specifying the ethical, social, and religious duties of all members of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya.

**Addressed To:**
The Shikshapatri is addressed to all Swaminarayan devotees — laypeople, monks, nuns, and leaders.

**Core Prohibitions (Selected):**
- Verse 11: "My devotees shall not consume meat."
- Verse 15: "My devotees shall not commit suicide even in extreme distress."
- Verse 26: "My devotees shall not oppress the weak."
- Verse 51: "Women devotees shall not associate with male ascetics."
- Verse 116: "Kings and rulers shall administer justice impartially."

**Daily Duties:**
- Perform morning and evening prayers (puja)
- Read/hear sacred texts (scripture recitation)
- Fast on Ekadashi (11th day of lunar month)

**Uniqueness:**
A copy of the Shikshapatri was presented to the Bodleian Library, Oxford in 1830 by Lieutenant Colonel John Grant, making it one of the earliest documented Indian religious texts at a Western university.

**Spiritual Significance:**
Bhagwan Swaminarayan declared: "The Shikshapatri is my visible form." It is treated with the same reverence as a living saint in the Sampradaya.

UniGuru Citation: Shikshapatri (original Sanskrit), Bhagwan Swaminarayan, 1826 CE; BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha translation

swaminarayan biography.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Bhagwan Swaminarayan: Life

---
title: Bhagwan Swaminarayan — Biography and Spiritual Journey
source: Harililamrut (Shatanand Muni); Bhaktachintamani (Nishkulanand Swami)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/swaminarayan-biography
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

Bhagwan Swaminarayan (born as Ghanshyam Pande on April 3, 1781 CE, Chhapaiya, Uttar Pradesh, India) is the founder and central deity of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya.

**Early Life:**
Born to Dharmadev (father) and Bhaktidevi (mother). His birth name was Ghanshyam. He was recognized from childhood as extraordinarily intelligent and spiritually advanced.

**Vanvicharan (Forest Wandering):**
At age 11, after his parents' death, Ghanshyam renounced home and undertook a seven-year walking pilgrimage across India (1792–1799). He traveled over 12,000 km on foot, visiting sacred sites, debating scholars, and practicing yoga under the name Nilkanth Varni.

**Meeting Swami Ramanand:**
In 1799, he met Swami Ramanand (Ramanuj Sampradaya saint) in Loj, Gujarat, and was initiated as a sadhu (monk). He was given the name "Sahajanand Swami" and "Narayan Muni."

**Becoming Acharya:**
In 1800, Ramanand Swami appointed Sahajanand Swami as his successor. The Sampradaya grew rapidly under his leadership across Gujarat.

**The Name "Swaminarayan":**
In 1800, at Faneni, he revealed to his disciples the divine name "Swaminarayan" — the combination of "Swami" (master) and "Narayan" (Supreme Being). This became the mahimamantra.

**Social Reforms:**
He abolished widow immolation (sati), female infanticide, and animal sacrifice. He educated women and uplift lower-caste communities against prevailing practices.

**Nirvana:**
Bhagwan Swaminarayan departed from the physical world on June 1, 1830 CE, at Gadhada, Gujarat. He is believed by his followers to have ascended to Akshardham (his divine abode) rather than dying.

UniGuru Citation: Harililamrut (Shatanand Muni); Bhaktachintamani (Nishkulanand Swami); BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha Historical Records

# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Swaminarayan Theology and Philosophy

---
title: Swaminarayan Theology — Vishishtadvaita and Beyond
source: Vachanamrut; Akshar-Purushottam Darshan; Vedantadesika tradition
url: https://www.baps.org/philosophy
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The theology of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya is rooted in Vishishtadvaita Vedanta (the qualified non-dualism) of Ramanuja, but significantly expands it by adding Akshar-Brahman as a distinct eternal entity.

**Traditional Vedanta Context:**
In Vishishtadvaita (Ramanuja):
- Brahman (God) = Supreme consciousness
- Chit (sentient) = Individual souls in Brahman's body
- Achit (non-sentient) = Matter in Brahman's body
- The world and souls are real but exist in and through God

**Swaminarayan's Innovation:**
Bhagwan Swaminarayan added a fifth eternal entity: Akshar-Brahman, distinguishing it from both Brahman's divine abode and the individual souls. This creates the "Panch-tattva" (five eternal elements):
1. Jiva (souls)
2. Ishvara (divine presiding deities)
3. Maya (primordial nature, cause of illusion)
4. Akshar (supreme divine abode and gateway)
5. Purushottam/Para-brahma (Supreme God — Swaminarayan)

**God's Sarvopari Nature:**
A central Swaminarayan theological claim is that Purushottam (Bhagwan Swaminarayan) is above and beyond all devas, including Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. He is not an avatara of Vishnu but the source from which all avataras emerge.

**Karma and Liberation:**
Liberation (moksha) in Swaminarayan theology means the soul attaining divine consciousness (brahmarup) through the grace of the living Akshar-Brahman on Earth, and ultimately residing eternally in Akshardham (the divine abode) in service to Purushottam.

UniGuru Citation: Vachanamrut Sarangpur-1; Akshar-Purushottam Darshan (BAPS Academic publication 2017)

swamini vato.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Swamini Vato (Gunatitanand Swami)

---
title: Swamini Vato — Spiritual Discourses of Gunatitanand Swami
source: Swamini Vato (compiled by sadhu disciples of Gunatitanand Swami)
url: https://www.baps.org/swamini-vato
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

Swamini Vato (Swami's Words / Swami's Discourses) is the primary secondary scripture of the BAPS Swaminarayan Sampradaya. It records the spiritual teachings of Gunatitanand Swami (1785–1867), who is regarded by BAPS as the first manifestation of Akshar-Brahman after Bhagwan Swaminarayan.

**About Gunatitanand Swami:**
- Born in Bhadra, Saurashtra, 1785 CE
- Initiated as a Swaminarayan sadhu by Bhagwan Swaminarayan himself
- Served as the mahant (head) of the Junagadh mandir for 40 years
- Continued delivering spiritual discourses constantly, recorded by his disciple sadhus

**Key Teachings from Swamini Vato:**

*On becoming Brahmarup (attaining divine consciousness):*
"One who associates with the Brahmaswarup (living Akshar on earth) and serves him with absolute devotion becomes brahmarup himself — and only then can one truly worship Purushottam."

*On the nature of Akshar:*
"Akshar is the abode of God, witness to all, and ever beyond maya. The sant who is brahmarup is the same Akshar in physical form."

*On sameness of the soul in all beings:*
"See God in all. The same consciousness that experiences in you experiences in every being. This vision is the mark of a true devotee."

*On liberation:*
"Moksha is not going to another world. It is the dissolution of all ego and the realization that the jiva is forever divine."

**Relationship to Vachanamrut:**
The Swamini Vato is considered the commentary and lived embodiment of the Vachanamrut's philosophical teachings.

UniGuru Citation: Swamini Vato (compiled from oral teachings of Gunatitanand Swami, transcribed by sadhu disciples); BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha study edition

vachanamrut core.content:
# Swaminarayan Knowledge Base — Vachanamrut (Complete Core)

---
title: Vachanamrut — The Principal Philosophical Text of Swaminarayan Sampradaya
source: Vachanamrut, Bhagwan Swaminarayan (compiled by Muktanand Swami et al.)
url: https://www.swaminarayan.org/vachanamrut
verification_status: VERIFIED
category: swaminarayan
---

## Content

The Vachanamrut (Vachana = Word, Amrut = Nectar) is the principal scripture of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya. It is a compilation of 273 discourses delivered by Bhagwan Swaminarayan between 1819–1829 CE, transcribed by his disciples and compiled into a canonical text.

**Structure:**
The 273 discourses are organized by location:
- Gadhada (I, II, III) — 76 + 67 + 39 discourses
- Sarangpur — 18 discourses
- Kariyani — 12 discourses
- Loya — 18 discourses
- Panchala — 7 discourses

## Content

The Vachanamrut (Vachana = Word, Amrut = Nectar) is the principal scripture of the Swaminarayan Sampradaya. It is a compilation of 273 discourses delivered by Bhagwan Swaminarayan between 1819–1829 CE, transcribed by his disciples and compiled into a canonical text.

**Structure:**
The 273 discourses are organized by location:
- Gadhada (I, II, III) — 76 + 67 + 39 discourses
- Sarangpur — 18 discourses
- Kariyani — 12 discourses
- Loya — 18 discourses
- Panchala — 7 discourses
- Vartal — 20 discourses
- Amdavad — 3 discourses
- Jetalpur — 4 discourses
- Bhugol-Khagol (extra additions)

3. **The Four Entities** — Para-brahma (Supreme God), Akshara-brahma (eternal abode), maya (illusion), and jivas (individual souls)

**Gadhada I-1 (The First Discourse):**
Swaminarayan asks: "What is the characteristic of an 'ekantik-bhakta'?"
Answer: One who possesses the four pillars of dharma, jnan, vairagya, and bhakti is an ekantik-bhakta (ultimate devotee).
